items.title: Intervention américaine contre le terrorisme au Mali ? «Nous n’attendons pas que d’autres viennent régler notre problème», affirme Abdoulaye Diop
items.description: Le ministre malien des Affaires etrangeres, Abdoulaye Diop, s'est exprime le 23 juillet, a Bamako, sur les informations du Washington Post selon lesquelles l'administration de Donald Trump envisage differentes options militaires contre le Groupe de soutien a l'islam et aux musulmans (JNIM), organisation affiliee a Al-Qaïda. S'exprimant lors d'une conference de presse organisee a l'occasion du Salon international de la defense et de la securite de Bamako (BAMEX 2026), le chef de la diplomatie malienne a indique que les autorites de son pays n'avaient recu, a ce stade, aucune communication officielle de Washington a ce sujet. « Je peux vous assurer que le Mali n'est pas assis en train d'attendre que d'autres viennent regler notre probleme », a declare Abdoulaye Diop. La souverainete d'abord Le ministre a rappele les principes qui fondent la politique etrangere malienne : le respect de la souverainete nationale, celui des choix operes par les autorites conformement aux interets de la population et la defense des interets du peuple malien. Ces principes constituent, selon lui, les conditions indispensables de toute cooperation avec Bamako. « Le Mali compte sur lui-meme, sur son intelligence, sur ses ressources, sur ses forces de defense et de securite et sur ses propres moyens pour regler les problemes », a-t-il affirme. Le dialogue comme prealable Abdoulaye Diop n'a toutefois pas ferme la porte a une cooperation securitaire internationale. Il a insiste sur la necessite d'une concertation prealable avec les autorites maliennes et sur le respect des besoins definis par le pays lui-meme. « Le minimum que nous puissions attendre d'un partenaire est qu'il vienne demander au Mali de quoi il a besoin », a-t-il souligne. Le Washington Post a rapporte, le 22 juillet, que l'administration Trump etudiait plusieurs options militaires visant le JNIM. Une eventuelle decision ferait du Mali un nouveau theâtre d'intervention militaire americaine depuis le retour de Donald Trump a la Maison Blanche. La nature et l'ampleur d'une eventuelle operation restent cependant indeterminees.

items.title: L'actu en dessin : que peut bien faire l'IA face aux incendies ?
items.description: La France est confrontée depuis plusieurs jours à des feux de forêts virulents, notamment dans le sud-ouest du pays. Quelque 32 foyers sont actuellement actifs, selon le ministre de l'Intérieur Laurent Nunez. Ces incendies qui font l'actualité sont aussi le thème central du dessin de la semaine signé Sanaga.

items.title: Burkina : 74,53 % du territoire national reconquis à la date du 30 juin 2026 (ministre de la Guerre)
items.description: BURKINA-ÉVALUATION-CONTRATS-OBJECTIFS-2026 Burkina : 74,53 % du territoire national reconquis à la date du 30 juin 2026 (ministre de la Guerre) Ouagadougou, 24 juil. 2026 (AIB)-Le ministre d'État, ministre de la Guerre et de la Défense patriotique, le Général de division Célestin Simporé, a annoncé, vendredi à Ouagadougou, à l'issue de l'évaluation à mi-parcours de son contrat d'objectifs, que 74,53 % du territoire national avait été reconquis à la date du 30 juin 2026. Selon le ministre, le Département de la Guerre a enregistré une progression dans la reconquête de l'intégrité du territoire national. Il a précisé qu'en décembre 2025, le taux de reconquête était de 73,56 %, et qu'il est passé à 74,53 % en juin 2026. « Il n'y a pas un endroit actuellement au Burkina où nos forces de défense et de sécurité ne peuvent pas aller. Nous pouvons aller partout et nous y allons. Le taux de 74,53 %, c'est le taux du territoire reconquis, pacifié, sécurisé, avec le retour progressif de la population et aussi de l'administration publique », a-t-il déclaré. Le ministre de la Guerre a ajouté que les zones, même les plus reculées, sont régulièrement ravitaillées. « Le retour de la population est une réalité. Nous coopérons et coordonnons avec les autres ministères pour assurer le retour progressif, soit par la route à travers des convois logistiques et le ravitaillement, soit par les airs pour le retour de l'administration », a soutenu le ministre. À l'issue de l'évaluation, le Département de la Guerre a obtenu, à la date du 30 juin 2026, un taux de réalisation de 53,14 % de son contrat d'objectifs à mi-parcours. Le Général de division Célestin Simporé a jugé ce taux assez satisfaisant, tout en soulignant qu'il ne fallait pas tomber dans l'autosatisfaction. « Nous ne pouvons qu'œuvrer et poursuivre, de sorte que, d'ici la fin de l'année, tout ce qui nous avait été instruit soit exécuté et que cela soit une réalité », a-t-il affirmé. Agence d'information du Burkina BAK/ata

items.title: UN urges evacuation of 6,000 sailors stranded in Hormuz strait
items.description: The United Nations on Friday called for the safe evacuation and repatriation of some 6,000 sailors stuck in a "dire humanitarian and human rights crisis" in the Strait of Hormuz. UN human rights chief Volker Turk called for urgent action to remedy the plight of thousands of seafarers caught up in the Middle East war, his spokeswoman Shabia Mantoo said.

items.title: Trump threatens a major escalation in Iran as the war nears the end of Month 5
items.description: The U.S. continued airstrikes in Iran while Tehran launched retaliatory attacks on U.S. targets in the Gulf, as President Trump said he was close to deciding whether to launch "a massive attack." (Image credit: AFP via Getty Images)

items.title: Too Scared for Sex
items.description: A little more than 20 years ago, Esther Perel was not yet an author or a podcast host. She was definitely not a household name. She was simply a psychotherapist with an observation: Many of her clients were in loving, supportive romantic relationships—and they weren’t having good sex, or any at all. At the time, the prevailing wisdom among psychologists was that a couple’s sex life was a reflection of their bond. Cultivate closeness, the thinking went, and you will feel safe to explore and to shed inhibitions. But Perel kept noticing the opposite: Emotional intimacy and desire seemed often to be at odds. That idea is fairly commonplace now—but that’s because Perel helped popularize it. In her 2006 book, Mating in Captivity , she described a kind of Greek tragedy that can thwart couples. Two people in heady early love, fearing uncertainty and aloneness, rush to get closer. But in doing so, they smother the spark of mystery that made the other person so desirable in the first place. Her book was a massive best seller, translated into more than 30 languages. Now she speaks in sold-out concert venues . Earlier this summer, The Invite , a film based partly on Mating ’s ideas, was released in theaters. (Olivia Wilde, the director and star, is a stan.) In May, when I went to an event Perel hosted in New York City, a line of attendees young and old stretched down the block, several carrying bags with one of her catchphrases: If you’re too busy for sex, you’re too busy . Perel has covered plenty of other subjects over the years. Still, her initial thesis has only gained relevance. Two decades after the book’s release, American culture is in a bit of a sensual drought—and people want to know why. According to some data, the average married couple have less sex now. Young adults are entering partnerships later and less often, and a striking number of them don’t even want to see sex on TV. But Perel has said she doesn’t want to focus on statistics. The endless measurement of sex—how often people are having it, with how many partners, starting at what age—strikes her as part of the problem: It’s a distinctly unsexy and terribly narrow way to understand desire. She’s interested, instead, in eroticism. Perel’s “erotic” can entail sex, but it doesn’t have to. It’s a state one enters into when they’re playful, intrigued, open to the unknown. And because it means venturing away from security, it always involves some risk. Perhaps the “ sex recession ,” then, is merely a symptom of something bigger: an eroticism shortage, resulting from a great discomfort with uncertainty. If so, the stakes would be much higher than they were in 2006. It would mean that people aren’t just falling out of lust with their partner but struggling to connect, to feel deeply, and to be present for their own life. “Today, the question is not so much How do you sustain desire in the long haul ,” Perel told me, “but How do we ignite it in the first place? ” Eroticism, as Perel sees it, can begin only when one has achieved a baseline sense of safety. She has said she came to this understanding in part because of her husband’s career as a psychologist working with torture survivors. Once, she asked him how he knows when someone has truly come back—when they’ve gone from being not dead to alive . He told her it’s when they can be creative. In order to explore, to be open to new ideas and experiences—all core to eroticism, in Perel’s view—people have to feel stable enough to let their guard down. They have to unclench. One doesn’t need to be a torture survivor to be in the habit of clenching: scanning for danger rather than being silly or raunchy or mischievous. “If you are in a state of vigilance,” Perel said, “you cannot be playful.” Americans do seem more vigilant than playful these days. According to one 2025 poll from the Badger Institute, a nonpartisan think tank, 66 percent of 18-to-29-year olds and 60 percent of 30-to-44-year olds agreed that “most people can’t be trusted.” Rates of avoidant attachment style—the tendency to keep distant, essentially—have been rising . Political priorities have become dominated by a yearning for security : protection against financial instability, crime, replacement. And politicians are rushing to acknowledge (or exploit) those fears. These are not the conditions in which eroticism thrives. [ Read: Why happy people cheat ] The result is that while readers have continued lapping up Mating in Captivity , Americans have broadly become worse at taking Perel’s advice. The central premise of the book is that partners can keep the erotic alive by accepting that they will never know each other completely. But the average couple have arguably become more intertwined. For a recent story on Americans’ particularly intense disdain for infidelity , I spoke with a sociologist who said that many people are disturbed by the idea that their partner could have needs and fantasies they don’t know about. Cheating is so despised, she argued, because it’s a reminder that no matter how much of yourself you share and how close you are to your partner, you’ll never be inside their mind. The fear of an unrecognizable partner is, at the moment, a notable obsession of popular culture. Take Strangers , Belle Burden’s mega-best-selling memoir about being left by her husband—suddenly, with no explanation. Over the course of the book, she replays their relationship in her head, searching for clues; she wonders whether something in his childhood set this in motion. When his previous behavior doesn’t lead a bread-crumb trail straight to his present actions, Burden makes it sound as creepy as if she’d been stuck in the house with her husband’s evil twin. On the Netflix horror series Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen , the primary source of dread is not the jump scares or the protagonist’s strange in-laws but the looming question of whether her fiancé isn’t who she thought he was. And in the April film The Drama , a man named Charlie (played by Robert Pattinson) learns something about his fiancée, Emma (Zendaya), that upends his understanding of her right before their wedding. Again and again he rewrites his vows, desperately searching for a cohesive story about his relationship. [ Read: The most tortured relationship in America ] You might think that in the past two decades, Americans would’ve grown accustomed to uncertainty. But Perel believes that many people are struggling to cope with that precarity. Why learn how, when you can use Google Maps to see exactly how delayed your train is, or ask ChatGPT to give you a breakdown of anything you don’t immediately understand? In a superficial sense, the world is more graspable and predictable than ever. Sometimes, though, that can make a person more afraid of ambiguity, more attached to the fiction that we can ever know what’s around the corner—or in our partner’s head. On top of that, Perel said, the kinds of communities and rituals people traditionally relied on when facing enigma and loss aren’t so common anymore. Fewer Americans are looking to religion to answer the big questions—how to live, what happens after death, why bad things happen to good people—or to let them wrestle with these issues communally. Fewer socialize regularly with their neighbors. The average family size keeps shrinking. A lot of people don’t know where else to look for certainty but their partner. So they cling for dear life. An aversion to the unknown isn’t a problem just for couples; it’s a problem for people without partners too, and for the culture at large. Single people are approaching first dates as if they’re interviews, ready to review the candidate, on the lookout for red flags. Or they’re not dating at all, sometimes even when they want sex or love. In my own reporting on romance, I hear from a lot of people on the defense against hurt feelings or wasted time. The weight of the world—the dismal news alerts, the rent bills, the doomscrolling—isn’t putting them in the mood for lighthearted banter or seductive dancing or organic inquisitiveness. Americans seem to be losing their taste for pleasure, sexual or not. In growing numbers, they’re short-circuiting their appetites with GLP-1s. They’re covering their homes in beige , following influencers who quietly tidy, and half-heartedly streaming mild comedies while they scroll. “The culture of these years,” my colleague Spencer Kornhaber wrote recently, “will be remembered for lots of things that dulled and distracted the senses: easygoing country music, friendly AI chatbots, ketamine nasal sprays.” And dulled senses are an enemy to eroticism. Perel believes that part of the problem is that people think they’re meant to heal first—to overcome burnout, to process trauma, to somehow refresh themselves until they’re shiny and new, ready for life to get more fun and more dynamic. But if you open the door to the uncertain and unpredictable, you let in suffering along with joy. The erotic isn’t what comes after pain; it’s a way of living with it. [ Read: Teens are forgoing a classic rite of passage ] Maybe that’s the path forward for couples and single people alike: to lean into the precarity, not because it’s easier or more pleasant but because they’re interested in where that will lead. Perel tells partners that they don’t need to try creating distance or adding novelty to their relationships; all they need to do is accept that it already exists. In fact, they should revel in the fact that they’ll always have more to discover about each other. (A lot of couples go out for dinner with friends and have fascinating, deep conversations, she told me—and then as soon as they’re alone again, “they switch to talking about who’s going to the ball game tomorrow.”) And what if single people started treating dates as adventures—opportunities to get to know someone new and do something different? Her advice, regardless of relationship status, is essentially this: Never give yourself over to numbness. In The Invite , Olivia Wilde’s film, two couples have a dinner party. One of those pairs seems electric with chemistry and presence; they laugh, touch each other with affection, speak aloud the observations that might have been politely contained. The other pair has had the life “seeped out of them,” as Perel put it to me. They’re on autopilot. They’re a little like a lot of people, in an age of blunted pleasure. The question of the film is whether the vitality of that first couple might wake them up. Spoiler alert: The movie is ambiguous on that point. But it does contain a hopeful note—much like The Drama , actually. Both films show their stunted couples attempting to start over, reenacting the beginning of their relationship, when everything was terrifyingly and beautifully undetermined. “I’m Emma,” Zendaya’s character says, and Charlie returns her handshake across the table. The viewer can only guess whether they’ll be brave enough to remain curious. When you buy a book using a link on this page, we receive a commission. Thank you for supporting The Atlantic .

Cameroun : report successif des élections, persistance de la crise, pilotage automatique du pays, le SDF frappe du poing sur la table
items.description: © DR Au cours d’une conférence de presse donnée à Yaoundé le 23 juillet 2026, le président national du Social Democratic Front, Joshua Osih, lève le ton pour dénoncer avec énergie la gestion de l’État tel que faite par le régime RDPC. Trois points retiennent l’attention dans le propos liminaire du chairman du Social Democratic Front délivré lors de la conférence de presse. Il s’agit en l’occurrence de la gouvernance du Cameroun, du report successif des élections, ainsi que de la gestion de la crise sécuritaire dans les régions du Nord-Ouest et du Sud-Ouest. En effet, Joshua Osih dénonce la gouvernance à distance de la République du Cameroun ainsi que le manque de transparence dans la gestion des ressources. Depuis six semaines, le chef de l’Etat est hors du territoire camerounais sans ‘’aucune image, aucune adresse, aucun retour annoncé’’. Et la vice-présidence ‘’créée à la hâte au nom de la continuité de l’Etat demeure vacante. Le Cameroun est une République en pilotage automatique’’, lance l’homme politique. Selon lui, nombre de souffrances de Camerounais sont la conséquence de ces manquements liés à la gouvernance. Lesquels sont visibles aussi à travers l’inefficacité pour répondre aux problèmes sociaux, l’absence de transparence dans la gestion de ressources comme les recettes pétrolières, fiscales et minières. ‘’Nous sommes en mesure de dire que les recettes fiscales, pétrolières et minières ne sont pas exactes. Le SDF réclame les vrais chiffres des recettes de l’Etat’’, lance-t-il en indiquant que le gouvernement peut régler les problèmes des Camerounais par un simple travail de gouvernance. Une démocratie taillée sur mesure Outre les problèmes de gouvernance, le SDF s’insurge contre les atteintes à la démocratie au pays. La réinstauration de la vice-présidence au Cameroun en excluant sa désignation par le vote populaire en est l’une des illustrations. Alors que le parti a quant à lui, proposé un amendement pour que le vice-président soit élu au même moment que le président et que l’un soit de l’ancien Cameroun français et l’autre de l’ancien Cameroun anglais, le parti au pouvoir a opté pour la nomination. Dans cet ordre de rejets, le parti a aussi déposé cinq propositions de loi qui n’ont pas été acceptées par la conférence des présidents de l’Assemblée nationale. Aussi le parti s’insurge contre le report successif des élections législatives et municipales et enjoint les autorités à respecter les échéances attendues en 2027. ‘’Ne touchez plus à la date des élections. Toute tentative de nouveau report de ces élections sous quelque prétexte que ce soit : sécurité, calendrier, finance, consensus, sera traitée par notre parti comme une confiscation inconstitutionnelle du mandat du peuple’’, prévient le chairman. Non à l’accoutumance à la crise anglophone Par ailleurs, le président du parti de la balance exprime la préoccupation de sa formation politique face à la persistance de la crise anglophone qui dure depuis 10 ans. D’après lui, 6 000 Camerounais ont été tués dans ce conflit. Il a entrainé environ 648 000 déplacés internes, avec des déplacés cumulés approchant 900 000 personnes. 80 000 personnes sont réfugiées au Nigeria. 600 000 enfants sont privés d’écoles. 2000 personnes sont en prison en lien avec cette crise. Environ 2 millions de personnes ont besoin d’assistance humanitaire. Toujours d’après lui, le Sud-Ouest a perdu son leadership cacaoyer et industriel. Les exportations et les investissements sont en baisse tandis que la croissance se perd d’année en année. Pourtant, à la veille de la présidentielle 2025, le SDF a fait des propositions pour une sortie de crise. Cet article Cameroun : report successif des élections, persistance de la crise, pilotage automatique du pays, le SDF frappe du poing sur la table est apparu en premier sur Journalducameroun.com .

items.title: Foot : Jürgen Klopp nommé sélectionneur de l’Allemagne (Officiel)
items.description: Jürgen Klopp a été nommé sélectionneur de l'Allemagne, succédant à Julian Nagelsmann, avec un contrat jusqu'à la Coupe du monde 2030 (Espagne, Portugal, Maroc), a annoncé vendredi Bernd Neuendorf, le président de la Fédération allemande de football (DFB). « Nous sommes heureux de pouvoir présenter Jürgen Klopp en tant que nouvel entraîneur », a-t-il déclaré lors d'une conférence de presse. « C'est idéalement le point culminant de ma carrière, de ma vie, de ma vie professionnelle. J'y mettrai toutes mes forces à disposition », a de son côté souligné le nouveau sélectionneur de la Mannschaft. Klopp a également prévenu que si la presse se déchaînait contre lui à l'avenir, il claquerait la porte : « Si demain vous dites : +il est merdique+, je suis parti ». Quatre jours après la piteuse élimination de la Mannschaft en 16es de finale de la Coupe du monde par le Paraguay, le sélectionneur allemand Julian Nagelsmann avait démissionné de son poste. Neuendorf s'était déplacé à New York avec l'un de ses vice-présidents, Hans-Joachim Watzke, pour de premières discussions avec Klopp, qui s'étaient conclues par un accord de principe. Mais Klopp était sous contrat depuis début 2025 avec Red Bull jusqu'à fin 2029 comme directeur mondial du football de la galaxie Red Bull, entreprise autrichienne de boissons énergisantes qui possède notamment Leipzig et Salzbourg, et des parts dans le Paris FC. Le feu vert est finalement venu du patron de Red Bull, Oliver Mintzlaff, qui a tenu une réunion avec les dirigeants de la DFB. Klopp est libéré sans indemnité de transfert, mais en contrepartie d'une simple donation d'un million d'euros à la fondation Wings for Life de Red Bull, dédiée à la recherche sur les lésions de la moelle épinière, a confirmé Bernd Neuendorf. Il a ajouté que Klopp mettra « complètement fin à ses fonctions (chez Red Bull) pour se concentrer pleinement sur le travail au sein de la Fédération ». À 59 ans, Jürgen Klopp se trouve devant le plus grand défi de sa carrière de technicien, marquée jusque-là par de nombreux succès sur les bancs de Dortmund (champion d'Allemagne 2011 et 2012) et de Liverpool (vainqueur de la Ligue des champions 2019, champion d'Angleterre 2020). Quadruple championne du monde (1954, 1974, 1990, 2014), l'Allemagne reste sur trois fiascos en phase finale de Coupe du monde, avec deux éliminations dès la phase de groupes en 2018 et 2022, et une sortie en 16es de finale cette année. Depuis sa demi-finale perdue à l'Euro 2016 contre la France, l'Allemagne n'a plus disputé un dernier carré d'un tournoi majeur, une décennie de disette inédite pour une nation également triple championne d'Europe (1972, 1980, 1996). AFP

items.title: Troops nab 5 suspected kidnappers in Yobe
items.description: By Ndahi Marama Troops of Sector 2 Operation HADIN KAI (OPHK), in conjunction with the Yobe State Hunters Group, have arrested five suspected kidnappers during a well-coordinated intelligence-led operation conducted on 23 July 2026 at Jegalari Village in Fune Local Government Area of Yobe State. This was disclosed in a press statement signed by the Acting Media Information Officer, Headquarters Joint Task Force North East Operation HADIN KAI, Captain Mohammed Goni, on Friday. “The operation, executed by troops of 233 Battalion deployed at Damaturu Military Cantonment, followed credible intelligence on the activities of the suspected criminal elements. “During the raid, troops recovered three locally fabricated AK-47 rifles, one locally made pistol, assorted ammunition, and four mobile phones believed to be linked to the suspects’ criminal activities. “The suspects are currently in military custody alongside the recovered exhibits for further investigation to ascertain their involvement in kidnapping and other related criminal activities. “Operation HADIN KAI remains resolute in its commitment to denying terrorists and other criminal elements freedom of action across the North East through sustained intelligence-driven operations. “The military urges members of the general public to remain vigilant and promptly report any suspicious persons, movements, or activities to the nearest security agency. Timely and credible information from law-abiding citizens remains critical to ongoing efforts to safeguard communities and enhance security across the region,” Goni explained. The post Troops nab 5 suspected kidnappers in Yobe appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Trump prendrait-il ses distances avec Netanyahou ?
items.description: La relation entre Donald Trump et Benjamin Netanyahou semble traverser une periode de fortes tensions. Depuis plusieurs semaines, le Premier ministre israelien chercherait a obtenir un nouvel entretien avec le president americain, sans succes. Selon The Wall Street Journal , la Maison Blanche ne serait pas pressee de fixer une date, Donald Trump cherchant a eviter une rencontre avec son allie historique. « Depuis plusieurs semaines, Netanyahou tente en vain d'obtenir un entretien avec le president americain. Trump ne souhaite pas voir Netanyahou et evite de le rencontrer », rapporte le quotidien americain. Cette situation marque un contraste avec les mois precedents. Durant son premier mandat, puis apres son retour a la Maison Blanche, Benjamin Netanyahou figurait parmi les dirigeants etrangers les plus recus dans le Bureau ovale, avec six rencontres en seulement un an et demi. Selon le Wall Street Journal , cette prise de distance serait notamment liee aux critiques exprimees par le Premier ministre israelien concernant une eventuelle vente d'avions de combat F-35 americains a la Turquie. Une opposition qui aurait irrite Donald Trump, favorable a un rapprochement avec Ankara. Les funerailles du senateur Lindsey Graham, proche du dirigeant israelien, auraient pu offrir une opportunite de renouer le dialogue entre les deux hommes. Mais la Maison Blanche aurait fait comprendre que la presence de Netanyahou n'etait pas attendue. a Washington, le contexte politique reste egalement defavorable au Premier ministre israelien. Le maire de New York, Zohran Mamdani, l'a recemment qualifie de « criminel de guerre », illustrant les critiques croissantes auxquelles Netanyahou fait face aux etats-Unis. Alors que les relations americano-israeliennes restent un pilier strategique au Moyen-Orient, cette distance inhabituelle entre Trump et Netanyahou souleve des interrogations sur l'evolution de leur alliance.

items.title: Grand Magal 2026 : la Société africaine de raffinage (SAR) réaffirme son engagement auprès de Touba
items.description: La Société africaine de raffinage (SAR) a apporté son soutien au khalife général des mourides dans le cadre des préparatifs du Grand Magal de Touba. Son directeur général, Habib Diop, a été reçu, jeudi 23 juillet 2026, par Serigne Mountakha Mbacké. En prélude au Grand Magal de Touba, la Société africaine de raffinage (SAR) a remis sa contribution au khalife général des mourides. C'est ce qu'a expliqué son directeur général, Habib Diop, en marge de son audience avec le guide religieux. Selon lui, il s'agit d'un appui symbolique en nature destiné à aider le khalife à faire face à certaines charges liées à l'organisation de l'événement. À l’en croire, le Grand Magal de Touba, qui rassemble chaque année des millions de fidèles, requiert l'accompagnement des entreprises publiques et parapubliques, en plus de celui de l'État du Sénégal. « Ces sociétés sont des démembrements de l'État », a-t-il souligné. Profitant de cette occasion, le directeur général de la SAR a également sollicité des prières auprès du khalife général des mourides. « Nous sommes venus solliciter des prières pour que le président de la République puisse réussir toutes les missions qui lui sont assignées », a-t-il déclaré, estimant que cela passe par la préservation de la stabilité et de la cohésion sociale. Birane Diop (Correspondant)

items.title: Banques : un déficit de liquidité de 147,2 MMDH du 15 au 22 juillet
items.description: Le déficit de liquidité bancaire moyen s'est allégé de 1,57% pour s'établir à 147,2 milliards de dirhams (MMDH) durant la période du 15 au 22 juillet, selon BMCE Capital Global Research (BKGR). Cette évolution intervient au moment où les avances 7 jours de Bank Al-Maghrib (BAM) affichent une progression de 17,75 MMDH à 55,4 MMDH, indique BKGR dans sa récente note « Fixed Income Weekly ». De leur côté, les placements du Trésor ont diminué, avec un encours quotidien maximal de 18,2 MMDH, contre 31,6 MMDH une semaine auparavant, précise la même source. Par ailleurs, le taux moyen pondéré (TMP) est resté stable à 2,25%, tandis que le MONIA (Moroccan Overnight Index Average : indice monétaire de référence au jour le jour, calculé sur la base des transactions de pensions livrées ayant comme collatéral les bons du Trésor) a reculé à 2,214%. Au cours de la prochaine période, la Banque Centrale devrait réduire le rythme de ses interventions sur le marché monétaire, ramenant le volume de ses avances à 7 jours à 44,1 MMDH. The post Banques : un déficit de liquidité de 147,2 MMDH du 15 au 22 juillet appeared first on Hespress Français - Actualités du Maroc .

items.title: Flooding, Climate Crisis: C’River lost 14,000 hectares of rainforest in 2025 — Conservator
items.description: By Ike Uchechukwu, CALABAR The Conservator of the Cross River National Park, Mr. Joseph Ntui, has raised alarm over the rapid depletion of Cross River’s rainforest, disclosing that about 14,000 hectares of tropical rainforest were lost in 2025 alone, resulting in the emission of an estimated eight million tonnes of carbon dioxide. Ntui gave the warning Thursday while speaking to Vanguard on the sidelines of the 4th Multi stakeholders’ Conference on Deforestation Cross River State with theme: ” Forest, People, and Policy-Shifting power abd Strategies for combating Deforestation orgsnised by We the people in Calabar Ntui described the rate of deforestation as a major environmental threat, warning that the continued destruction of the forest is endangering biodiversity, worsening climate change and contributing to recurring floods in the state. According to him, global research data indicate that the destruction of about 14,000 hectares of rainforest last year released approximately eight million tonnes of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. “The Cross River forest, which is globally recognised as a refuge for endangered wildlife, is disappearing at an alarming rate. “As the forest disappears, species such as the Cross River gorilla, Nigeria-Cameroon chimpanzee, forest elephants and pangolins are losing their habitats, making them more vulnerable to poaching and eventual extinction,” he said. The conservator explained that part of the Cross River rainforest was designated a national park in 1991, covering about 4,000 square kilometres across Akamkpa, Boki, Obanliku Local Government Areas of the state. He said the park’s management had continued to intensify efforts to safeguard the protected area through armed patrols, community engagement, scientific research and collaboration with conservation organisations. According to him, the park currently has about 250 armed rangers deployed to protect biodiversity and forest resources from illegal activities. “We cannot protect these resources alone. That is why we work closely with host communities, conservation partners and researchers. We also carry out conservation education to sensitise people on the dangers of deforestation,” he stated. Also Speaking , the Executive Director of We the People, Ken Henshaw, said the state’s existing forestry law had become obsolete and no longer reflected emerging environmental challenges, particularly climate change. He said participants at the previous edition of the conference resolved to review the Cross River State Forestry Law 2010, leading to the production of a document titled Recommended Reviews for the Cross River Forestry Law 2010. According to Henshaw, the recommendations were developed through months of consultations involving conservation groups, legal experts, academics, community representatives and other stakeholders to address gaps in the law and strengthen forest governance. He said, “The existing forestry law says almost nothing about climate change because it is an obsolete law. We examined the gaps and developed consensus-based recommendations to modernise the law and bring it in line with current realities.” Henshaw noted that despite the state’s forest conservation policies over the past two decades, deforestation had continued at an alarming rate, with illegal logging evolving into an organised enterprise involving local collaborators and foreign interests. He also identified illegal mining as a growing threat to the state’s forests, saying it had contributed to environmental degradation, pollution of water sources, destruction of farmlands and insecurity in forest communities. The post Flooding, Climate Crisis: C’River lost 14,000 hectares of rainforest in 2025 — Conservator appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: La lettre de Biaka Tedang Djoret à la Commission de l’UA, à la recherche d’une réconciliation exigeante au Tchad
items.description: Le Statisticien-économiste, Biaka Tedang Djoret, un citoyen tchadien auteur de Sans armes, de la statistique à la lutte citoyenne et Co-auteur de Pour un Tchad nouveau , dans une lettre ouverte, en date du 21 juillet 2026, s'adresse au président de la Commission de l'Union africaine. Dans ce document, parvenu à Wakat Séra ce vendredi 24 juillet 2026, M. Djoret évoque le processus de réconciliation nationale dont le Tchad s’apprête à ouvrir et demande un accompagnement conforme aux instruments de l’Union africaine. « Monsieur le Président, Je vous écris en citoyen tchadien, sans mandat d’aucun parti ni d’aucun gouvernement — un citoyen sans armes, qui écrit sans haine. Mon pays s’apprête à ouvrir un processus de réconciliation nationale et de justice transitionnelle, issu de l’Accord de Doha et du Dialogue national inclusif et souverain (DNIS) de 2022. Un procéssus qui rentre dans le registre d’une justice transitionnelle offerte dont la pièce maîtresse sera une loi créant une commission Vérité. Ce moment peut être historique pour mon pays. Il peut aussi rejoindre la longue liste de nos processus inaboutis. La différence tiendra aux conditions — et c’est à leur sujet que je sollicite l’Union Africaine. Je joins à la présente des documents publics : un registre citoyen des engagements non exécutés, dont chaque ligne est sourcée ; une doctrine citoyenne, articulée avec les douze exigences formulées par près de trente organisations de la société civile tchadienne ; et une note d’architecture pour la loi à venir. Trois faits, parmi d’autres, y sont documentés : le Dialogue national de 2022 avait fixé le mandat présidentiel à « cinq ans renouvelable une seule fois, sans possibilité de révision constitutionnelle » — ce verrou, inscrit dans la Constitution de 2023 adoptée par référendum, a été supprimé par voie parlementaire en 2025 ; le même Dialogue avait exigé un mécanisme de suivi « de manière immédiate » — le Cadre indépendant institué à cet effet a été dissous par décret n° 2211/PR/2025 du 16 septembre 2025 ; et les indemnisations dues aux victimes du régime Habré, ordonnées par les Chambres africaines extraordinaires créées sous l’égide de l’Union, demeurent partielles, sans calendrier publié. J’ajoute que la démarche citoyenne que je porte a formulé publiquement une offre de sortie encadrée par la vérité et la restitution de l’Etat patrimonialisé, strictement alignée sur la Politique de justice transitionnelle de l’Union : amnistie générale et croisée pour les infractions politiques et d’opinion, gagnée par cinq actes vérifiables de l’État ; clémence conditionnée à la vérité pour les crimes de sang — jamais d’impunité totale pour les crimes contre l’humanité ; règlement par la restitution pour les crimes économiques, chaque avoir restitué abondant le Fonds des réparations. Nous n’attendons pas de l’Union qu’elle parle à notre place; nous lui demandons la cohérence d’un accompagnement auquel le Tchad a librement souscrit : Au titre du Fonds fiduciaire de l’Union pour les réparations de l’affaire Habré : que l’Union Africaine publie l’état de ce Fonds et le calendrier de son opérationnalisation. L’affaire Habré fut une fierté africaine — la première fois qu’un continent jugeait l’un de ses anciens chefs d’État. Que ses victimes attendent encore leurs réparations est une ligne de notre registre qui concerne l’Union elle-même ; permettez-nous, avec respect, de vous la soumettre symétriquement ; Au titre du Conseil de paix et de sécurité : que soient désignés, parmi les figures illustres de la société civile et des autorités traditionnelles et religieuses du continent — dans l’esprit du Groupe des Sages —, des observateurs appelés à siéger, dans la Commission à créer, et à recevoir, en qualité de témoins, les déclarations d’acceptation de principe des mouvements non signataires de l’Accord de Doha ; Au titre de la Charte africaine de la démocratie, des élections et de la gouvernance : que les organes compétents de l’Union apprécient, à la lumière de la Charte, la conformité de la révision constitutionnelle de 2025 supprimant la limitation des mandats avec les principes d’alternance démocratique et de limitation de la concentration du pouvoir ; Au titre de la Politique de justice transitionnelle de l’Union (2019) : que tout accompagnement du processus tchadien soit adossé aux standards que l’Union s’est donnés — mécanismes indépendants, approche centrée sur les victimes, garanties de non-répétition —, y compris les verrous détaillés dans la note jointe, le dépôt-miroir des archives et du rapport final auprès de la Commission africaine des droits de l’homme et des peuples et le calendrier parallèle de réformes engagées pendant le processus ainsi qu’un appui conséquent aux organisations de la société civile et aux associations des victimes ; Au titre de l’accompagnement sécuritaire : que l’appui international aux forces tchadiennes ne renforce plus uniquement leurs capacités opérationnelles. Des travaux indépendants — du rapport n° 298 d’International Crisis Group (2021) à l’étude de l’Institut Egmont (2025) — décrivent une institution structurée par des lignes de fracture communautaires, des inégalités de traitement et des circuits de loyauté personnelle. L’accompagnement devrait soutenir aussi la représentativité, la professionnalisation, la discipline, la redevabilité, le contrôle parlementaire et la prévention des abus : une armée efficace à l’extérieur mais contestée à l’intérieur – parce que sources de conflits et de violence – ne peut pas être durablement un facteur de stabilité régionale ; Au titre du recouvrement des avoirs : que l’Union et ses partenaires appuient la coopération internationale nécessaire au volet économique de la justice transitionnelle tchadienne — entraide judiciaire, initiative StAR, registres des bénéficiaires effectifs — afin que l’argent restitué revienne au Fonds des réparations ; Au titre du suivi et de l’écoute : que l’Union appuie la recréation d’un cadre indépendant de suivi des engagements, protégé par la loi ; qu’elle désigne en son sein un point focal chargé de suivre le processus tchadien, en lien avec la Commission africaine des droits de l’homme et des peuples ; et qu’un canal formel d’écoute des victimes et de la société civile tchadienne soit ouvert auprès de ses services compétents. Monsieur le Président, L’Union Africaine a, avec la crise tchadienne, une histoire particulière — chacun s’en souvient, et l’Union Africaine sait mieux que quiconque ce qu’il en a coûté, jusque dans ses propres rangs, de défendre l’esprit du dialogue au Tchad. Je ne convoque pas ce souvenir pour raviver une querelle, mais pour souligner une opportunité : le processus qui s’ouvre offre à l’UA l’occasion d’écrire, au Tchad précisément, une autre page — celle d’un accompagnement exigeant, fidèle à ses propres textes, dont les victimes seraient les premières bénéficiaires. Le Tchad n’a pas besoin qu’on parle à sa place. Il a besoin que ceux qui l’accompagnent lisent ce qu’il a signé — et l’aident à s’y tenir. C’est toute ma demande. Je vous prie d’agréer, Excellence Monsieur le Président de la Commission, l’expression de ma très haute considération. » Biaka Tedang Djoret contact@djoret-biakatedang.com | www.djoret-biakatedang.com Cliquez ici pour lire les document associés à la lettre ouverte Pièces jointes : Registre des engagements non exécutés ; La bataille des conditions: doctrine citoyenne de la justice transitionnelle ; Tableau de correspondance des 12 exigences et des 10 verrous ; Architecture citoyenne de la loi CVPRR ; Série des quatre articles. Copies : Secrétaire général des Nations Unies ; Haut-Commissariat des Nations Unies aux droits de l’homme ; PNUD ; Bureau d’appui à la consolidation de la paix (PBSO/PBF) ; Union européenne ; Communauté économique des États de l’Afrique centrale (CEEAC), en sa qualité de garant de l’accord de Kinshasa ; Organisation internationale de la Francophonie ; Commission africaine des droits de l’homme et des peuples ; Human Rights Watch ; Amnesty International ; International Crisis Group ; Institut d’études de sécurité. L’article La lettre de Biaka Tedang Djoret à la Commission de l'UA, à la recherche d'une réconciliation exigeante au Tchad est apparu en premier sur Wakat Séra .

items.title: Newcastle sign Monaco midfielder Bamba on five-year deal
items.description: Monaco’s French midfielder Aladji Bamba has signed a five-year deal with Premier League club Newcastle, both teams confirmed on Friday. The deal is reported to be worth 35 million euros ($40m) with the Principality side to receive a percentage of the future sale of the 20-year-old. “I was very excited when I heard Newcastle were interested in signing me,” said Bamba, who becomes the club’s fourth signing this summer. “I know about the history of the club and I’ve visited the stadium already, which is amazing. I can’t wait to be on the pitch there and hope to have some great times with the fans. “I’m excited to test myself in a new league, the biggest league in the world. I learned a lot last season and now I feel ready for this new opportunity. I will give my all for this club.” Bamba joined Monaco in 2021 but has played just 25 matches — 18 in Ligue 1, two in the French Cup and five in the Champions League. The Riviera outfit finished fifth in Ligue 1 last season. Newcastle manager Eddie Howe welcomed the arrival of “another exciting, young signing who we’re really looking forward to working with”. “Aladji adds depth and a range of qualities to our midfield options, with huge potential for growth,” said Howe. “His development since breaking through at Monaco has been really impressive and something we believe we can help him build on.” Newcastle finished 12th in the Premier League last season. AFP The post Newcastle sign Monaco midfielder Bamba on five-year deal appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Arewa group backs INEC chair’s call for direct funding from Federation Account
items.description: The Arewa Development and Advocacy Network (ADNAN) has endorsed the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) Chairman’s call for the Commission to receive direct funding from the Federation Account, urging the National Assembly to urgently enact reforms that would guarantee the electoral body’s financial autonomy. In a statement issued on Friday by its Convener, Dr. Ahmadu Mustapha, the group described the proposal as a necessary step towards strengthening Nigeria’s democracy, arguing that INEC’s continued dependence on the annual appropriation process undermines its constitutional independence and operational effectiveness. The endorsement follows remarks made by INEC Chairman, Prof. Joash Amupitan, during the public presentation of Shadows: Protest Essays on Africa’s Most Consequential Country (1999–2023), a two-volume publication authored by ThisDay Managing Director, Eniola Bello, in Abuja on Thursday. According to the INEC Chairman, although the 1999 Constitution places the Commission on the first-line charge, the existing funding arrangement does not provide genuine financial independence. He explained that the envelope budgeting system and annual appropriation process compel the Commission to seek funding releases through the National Assembly, creating delays that affect election planning and operational efficiency. Backing the position, ADNAN said direct access to the Federation Account would provide predictable and non-discretionary funding needed for the Commission to conduct credible elections and discharge its constitutional responsibilities without unnecessary financial constraints. The group noted that INEC’s mandate extends beyond presidential and National Assembly elections to governorship and state assembly polls, voter registration, political party regulation and the maintenance of the national voter register. It argued that such extensive responsibilities require a funding model that guarantees certainty and independence. It further warned that inadequate and unpredictable funding exposes election management bodies to logistical challenges, delayed deployment of election materials, insufficient personnel training and declining public confidence in the electoral process. To address the challenge, ADNAN called on the National Assembly to amend the Electoral Act and relevant fiscal laws to provide direct funding for INEC from the Federation Account. It also urged the Federal Government to treat the Commission’s financial autonomy as a constitutional imperative, comparable to the autonomy granted to the judiciary and other independent institutions. The group further appealed to the executive and legislature to engage constructively with INEC’s proposal, while calling on civil society organisations, the media, youth groups and political parties to support efforts aimed at strengthening the Commission’s institutional and financial independence. ADNAN also encouraged the INEC Chairman to sustain advocacy for the proposal and work with relevant stakeholders to secure the legislative and administrative reforms needed ahead of future election cycles. The organisation maintained that the credibility of Nigeria’s elections depends largely on the independence, capacity and financial stability of the electoral umpire. The post Arewa group backs INEC chair’s call for direct funding from Federation Account appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: La clim’ qui chauffe la planète (Par Babacar Guèye DIOP)
items.description: Jadis hors de portée de nombreux Sénégalais, le climatiseur n’est plus aujourd’hui un luxe à Dakar. Face à des températures de plus en plus élevées, il s’est installé dans les habitudes quotidiennes. Dans les maisons, les bureaux, les commerces, les hôtels et certaines administrations, la climatisation est devenue un refuge contre la chaleur. Le climatiseur apporte un confort indispensable pendant les périodes de forte chaleur, mais il augmente également la demande énergétique, contribue aux émissions de gaz à effet de serre et rejette de la chaleur dans des villes déjà fragilisées par l’urbanisation. Au Sénégal, cette question devient de plus en plus préoccupante. Dakar connaît depuis plusieurs années une transformation rapide de son paysage urbain : multiplication des constructions, recul des espaces verts, forte densité du bâti et augmentation du trafic automobile. Dans ce contexte, le recours à la climatisation progresse. Selon l’Agence nationale de la statistique et de la démographie (Ansd), la production d’électricité a augmenté de 13,9 % au premier trimestre 2026. Les ventes d’électricité en basse tension, qui concernent principalement les ménages et les petits consommateurs, ont également enregistré une hausse de 27,3 % en décembre 2025 par rapport à la même période de l’année précédente. Avec la hausse des températures, la demande d’électricité destinée au rafraîchissement des bâtiments devient un enjeu majeur pour le système énergétique sénégalais. Le phénomène dépasse largement les frontières nationales. Selon l’Agence internationale de l’énergie (Aie), le nombre de climatiseurs dans le monde pourrait passer d’environ 2 milliards aujourd’hui à plus de 5 milliards d’ici à 2050 si les tendances actuelles se poursuivent. L’organisation estime également que la climatisation représente déjà près de 10 % de la consommation mondiale d’électricité et qu’elle constituera l’un des principaux moteurs de l’augmentation de la demande énergétique dans les prochaines décennies. La climatisation pose aussi une autre question environnementale : celle des fluides frigorigènes. Certains gaz utilisés dans les appareils, notamment les hydrofluorocarbures (Hfc), ont un pouvoir de réchauffement climatique bien supérieur à celui du CO₂. Le Programme des Nations unies pour l’environnement (Pnue) estime que la réduction progressive de ces gaz, prévue par l’amendement de Kigali au protocole de Montréal, pourrait éviter jusqu’à 0,4 °C de réchauffement climatique d’ici à 2100. Pour autant, il ne s’agit pas de condamner la climatisation. Dans un contexte de hausse des températures, elle joue un rôle important pour protéger les populations vulnérables, les travailleurs exposés à la chaleur et certains services essentiels. Le véritable défi est d’éviter une dépendance incontrôlée. Cela passe par une autre manière de construire et d’aménager les villes : préserver les arbres, développer les espaces verts, favoriser la ventilation naturelle, utiliser des matériaux adaptés au climat et encourager l’utilisation d’équipements moins énergivores. Le Sénégal dispose d’un potentiel important avec le développement des énergies renouvelables, notamment le solaire. Mais produire davantage d’électricité ne suffira pas si la demande continue d’augmenter sans transformation de nos modes de consommation. Demain, la bataille climatique ne se jouera pas seulement dans les grandes conférences internationales. Elle se jouera aussi dans nos maisons, derrière chaque télécommande de climatiseur. Car le véritable défi demeure : comment rechercher la fraîcheur sans contribuer à réchauffer davantage la planète ? bgdiop@lesoleil.sn

items.title: Why So Much Millennial Fiction Is Boring
items.description: Perhaps every generation believes that it is unsuited to its own era. As an elder Millennial, I can’t help but see how perfectly primed my cohort was for feelings of inadequacy and distress. Raised on the standard American diet of promise and progress, many in my micro-generation arrived in high school just in time for the Columbine shootings to undermine our sense of safety. As many of us entered the age of military fitness, the Twin Towers fell and the United States embarked on a forever war. The Great Recession greeted our arrival into the job market, and coronavirus-pandemic lockdowns coincided with the years when a lot of us were becoming parents . Now our early middle age is marked by inflation, environmental chaos, and AI’s potentially existential threat to job stability. Even if most generations complain about discontent, mine may have a particularly strong case for it. What’s a fiction writer to do with all of that joylessness? Plenty of authors in generations past made it their subject. Anna Karenina , Emma Bovary , and Tess Durbeyfield didn’t lead lives of unmitigated bliss. But contemporary fiction has a particular task: to explain and make interesting the deep disaffection of this era. It isn’t sadness, exactly, that lurks inside the hearts of Millennials and their similarly cursed younger siblings Gen Z, but something closer to lack—of security, of certainty, of sensual engagement with the world. The novelist who wants to write about how young people (barely) get by in the world today has to explain why all of this overwhelm often translates into paralysis—without boring readers to distraction. Like its cousin the internet novel , the novel of alienation needs a stylistic foil to make it sing. Methodically charting the flatness of ennui won’t get you very far; when your readers feel as listless as your characters do, they’re likely to reach for their phone in search of dopamine hits. Instead, you have to jolt your audience into an uneasy kinship. In I Want You to Be Happy , Jem Calder’s first novel, the author fails at this delicate task, and not spectacularly but anemically. His premise is simple: Chuck, a 35-year-old recently unengaged copywriter, meets the 23-year-old Joey, a barista with no better career prospects in view. For a few months, they casually date—a classic “situationship,” dogged on both sides by ambivalence and occasional apathy. Eventually, the relationship comes to an impasse, and each has to decide whether it’s worth their energy to carry on. But energy is scarce in Calder’s writing. Chuck and Joey have the kind of colorless sex that Calder describes in a monotone: “He asked her the universal male sex-act-recipient questions.” The couple also have very little to say to each other in between beddings. One date features “some discussion comparing alcohol and coffee,” and during a long weekend in the country, among their most intimate remarks is: “The showers here are good.” [ Read: These Millennials can’t get out of their head ] Both partners are flailing: Chuck is put on a performance-improvement plan at his firm for poor productivity and frequent hangovers, and Joey cannot afford to buy a cocktail or live anywhere better than the squalid bedroom that she rents in a shared house. But they regard their positions with little emotion beyond self-pity. In fact, that’s how they look at all of life. Neither has any hobbies or intellectual pursuits: Nobody so much as waters a plant or visits a museum. Both claim that they want to be a writer, and these dreams generate some intriguing tension, but readers rarely get a glimpse of what they produce. Joey reads two books that we know of, a work of poetry she admires for reasons that she does not explain and another described only as “boring.” Although I Want You to Be Happy is set in present-day London, no one probes the sorry state of the post-Brexit British government, except to comment vaguely that life is too expensive and friends are leaving the city. Most important, no one displays even a wisp of curiosity. The inner lives of Chuck and Joey are as untextured as the phone screens they tap incessantly. Curiosity, perhaps more than any other characteristic, is what pulls a reader along for a fictional ride. A character may simply want to find out how their new neighbor is getting along ( The Great Gatsby ), or whether they’ve married the right person ( Middlemarch ), or whether they’ll catch the damn whale (you know the one). But along the way, a single musing can turn into many more, like branching rows of dominoes setting off new lines of inquiry about the world. Even in novels centered on Millennial inaction or stasis, such as Pond , by Claire-Louise Bennett, a kind of attentiveness is necessary to advance beyond rhetorical water-treading. Other writers have successfully captured the malaise afflicting the under-45 crowd and offered nutrient-dense characters. In her clever 2021 debut novel, Three Rooms , the British writer Jo Hamya uses social realism to chronicle the downward mobility of a bright young thing, an Oxford postdoc researcher who moves from university-adjacent housing to an acquaintance’s couch and then to her childhood bedroom, her future dimming a little bit more with each domestic downgrade. By setting the action in 2018 and 2019, during the prolonged debacle of Brexit negotiations, Hamya links the degradation of the college degree to the stagnation—in the government, the economy, the culture—that has impeded progress for an entire generation. Hilary Leichter’s 2023 novel, Terrace Story , is similarly concerned with the indignities of the housing market, but this novelist employs a different artistic lever to keep the wheels turning: She uses dark fantasy to set our world in grim relief. A magical terrace appears inside the apartment of a young family struggling to pay the rent, offering them a chance to see how the other half lives. But it also cleaves the family apart, sending them spinning toward strange futures on a warped planet Earth. Despite these complications, Terrace Story delivers a brilliant contemporary fable that chronicles how young people stuck in the saddest timeline can still make meaning. Not every successful novel of alienation turns on financial deprivation. In fact, the central problem in Vincenzo Latronico’s Perfection , which dispenses with dialogue entirely in depicting two vacant creative professionals, is the comfort and style of their apartment. Scandinavian armchairs and monstera houseplants are all that this couple have: The accumulation of purely superficial identity markers, which they offer up online as proof of some mythical, unrealized happiness, leaves them weary and adrift. Using hyper-specific examples (Kraftwerk LPs, the German migrant crisis of 2015 and 2016), Latronico creates a book that functions as a time capsule—one that beautifully realizes his characters’ disenchantment, precisely imagining what it’s like to become trapped by an artificial, digital version of oneself. [ Read: The unhappy literary families of the internet age ] Some recent satires fail to reach the same heights of ingenuity, but they still have the consolations of humor. In her 2021 novel, Fake Accounts , Lauren Oyler’s snappy bons mots thoughtfully ridicule our contemporary culture of disdain even as they indulge in it to a wearying degree. And in the slightly exasperating Banal Nightmare , from 2024, Halle Butler has a knack for capturing the ludicrous way that therapy-speak has turned a generation of wobbly neurotics from patients into self-ordained shrinks. Calder, however, lacks even a hint of humor. I Want You to Be Happy has the ambience of a dentist’s waiting room: lots of barely stifled dread accompanied by relentless scrolling. Calder may have mistaken writing about boredom for writing boredom. His prose about phone usage emphasizes physical acts (“He gaze-unlocked his phone, thumbed to his conversation with Joey, absently flexed his free hand open and closed beside the screen, then relocked his device”) rather than immersing us in the stuff that has his characters (and so many of us) in its thrall. Cellphones are, to be fair, a problem for novelists: Their presence eliminates the face-to-face dynamism that so much classic literature uses to its advantage. (Just imagine Mr. Darcy proposing in text-speak.) But phones are also surprisingly similar to novels—both ask people to doggedly follow and emotionally invest in the lives of strangers. They are a narrative boon to any writer interested in human longing. All of which makes Calder’s book a major missed opportunity. The novel’s lack of interest in what’s going on inside its characters’ phones becomes indistinguishable from a disinterest in what’s happening in their minds. Designing purely surface-level characters to comment on the ways that technology homogenizes culture is the kind of exercise that can create only distance between readers and characters. I want to understand alienation; I don’t want to feel alienated—and nothing more—by reading. Young life is certainly exhausting and at times degrading and disconcerting, but is it really as boring as it is in this book? As a reasonably settled 42-year-old, I might not be the best person to ask. But as a reader, I’m certain that if novelists want to make clear just how disappointed they are in their life and the world, they’re going to have to do it with flair. Because they’re competing with the dancing pixels, and the pixels are winning.

items.title: The Social Security Reckoning Is Finally Coming
items.description: A mericans have been hearing about the looming demise of Social Security for decades. The number of old people receiving benefits has been increasing much more quickly than the number of working-age adults whose taxes pay for those checks. Yet politicians have let the problem fester for so long that the public could be forgiven for thinking that the crisis would never actually arrive. But it’s arriving. For the past 16 years, Social Security has paid out more than it has taken in. This has required dipping into its trust fund, built up over the decades when the Baby Boomers were in their peak earning years. That fund is on track to run out by 2032. When it does, benefits will have to be cut by an expected 22 percent across the board to line up with the tax revenue that funds them. Pretty soon, politicians won’t be able to kick this can down the road anymore. Senators elected this November will be in office in 2032. So will the next president. They will have no choice but to come up with a way to save the federal government’s largest and most popular program, lest they be blamed for its partial collapse. The available solutions are politically tricky but not particularly mysterious. They involve raising taxes, increasing the national debt, and cutting benefits, or some mix of the three. Even if our leaders settle on a way to keep Social Security solvent, however, they are unlikely to address the deeper problem underlying the budgetary one: A program designed to reduce poverty has morphed into a transfer of wealth to the richest members of our society. I n 1935, when the Social Security Act was signed into law, the average person entering the workforce could be expected to die at about 65 years old. Those who lived past that age tended to be too disabled to work, leaving them reliant on their children, if not a local charity or poorhouse. Social Security was designed to ameliorate this problem by levying a tax on workers and their employers, and paying benefits to the elderly roughly in proportion to how much they earned during their career. When the checks first went out, in 1940, the average recipient received $22.60 a month, or about $550 in today’s dollars. Such payments did not require too much sacrifice—just a 1 percent tax on the first $3,000 of a person’s earnings, matched by their employer—because there were many times more workers than there were beneficiaries. The program ballooned over time. Congress voted repeatedly to expand benefits faster than inflation. But the biggest changes weren’t purposeful. Because benefits are proportional to what a retiree earned during their working years, America’s increasing salaries necessarily made the program grow. So did rising life expectancy. The average 65-year-old now has about 20 years left to live, up from 13 years in 1940. Meanwhile, the pool of workers paying into the system has not kept pace. Fertility rates have fallen, and fewer babies mean fewer workers in the future. The number of workers for each beneficiary has gone down 24 percent since 1990, as more and more is being asked of a smaller share of the population. [ Idrees Kahloon: An oligarchy of old people ] The unfortunate fact is that Social Security was never guaranteed to work forever. The tax formula is simple: Each worker, along with their employer, pays 6 percent of their income, up to $184,500. The benefit formula is not. The Social Security Administration takes your highest-earning 35 years of work and calculates your average monthly earnings from those years, adjusted upward for subsequent growth in average wages. A progressive formula determines how much of that you’ll be paid monthly: Assuming that you elect to start receiving benefits at age 67, you’ll get 90 percent of the first $1,300, 32 percent of the next $6,500, and 15 percent of the remaining. The benefit side of the equation and the tax side of the equation do not add up to the same number. Thanks to the wage adjustment and long life expectancies, most people receive substantially more money than they paid in. In the aggregate, revenues have lagged behind benefits. “There’s no reason those formulas would be compatible with each other,” Andrew Biggs, a former deputy commissioner of the Social Security Administration, told me. The only reason they ever were was that America used to have enough workers to support its population of retirees. Preventing the program’s insolvency is a math problem that involves tweaking the tax side, the benefit side, or both, until they equal each other. This is not conceptually difficult. Steven Kull, a political psychologist at the University of Maryland, runs a poll every so often in which he shows participants the possible reforms, telling them how much each change closes the funding gap. “The biggest theme is that people solve the problem,” Kull told me, with “majorities taking steps that eliminate most or all of the shortfall.” Respondents tend to be confused as to why their elected leaders haven’t fixed the issue. “The common answer is, That wasn’t that hard. What’s all the fuss about? ” The problem is not that the math is difficult, but that every possible move comes with downsides. “Social Security reform is ultimately about breaking promises,” Biggs said. Either you raise taxes above the rate that people are used to, or you cut benefits for people who were counting on them. Historically, Democrats have preferred the former path, proposing to raise taxes on the well-off to pay for the program’s shortfall while leaving benefits alone. Republicans have traditionally wanted to shrink the program—although typically in a somewhat progressive manner. After President George W. Bush’s unpopular privatization push failed, for example, he proposed a slow flattening of the program by making the benefit formula less generous for all but the bottom 30 percent of recipients. The Democratic approach has always been more politically popular. Donald Trump was the first Republican to accept that fact. In 2015, as he marched to the Republican nomination, he promised not to touch Social Security, breaking with GOP orthodoxy and thrilling the base. Ever since, the Republican discussion on saving the program has had a distinctly muted tone. A March 2024 white paper by the Republican Study Committee—a caucus that includes most House Republicans—proposed raising the retirement age and “gradually moving towards a flat benefit.” Neither Trump nor Speaker of the House Mike Johnson commented on the proposal. Project 2025, produced by the Heritage Foundation, was more shrewd. The 900-page document contained advice for the future administration on how to properly regulate the shrinking population of greater sage grouse, but it contained no guidance on Social Security. Democratic plans still revolve around raising taxes while keeping benefits constant or even increasing them. But they now tend to include a feature designed to keep the party’s upper-middle-class base happy: the “doughnut hole.” Many of the current Democratic plans uncap the payroll tax, but typically only for earnings above $400,000, meaning that dollars earned between $185,000 and $400,000 would go untaxed, and everyone who makes less than $185,000 would pay the highest payroll tax rate of all. Representative John Larson, a Connecticut Democrat, has introduced many Social Security–reform plans over his 14 terms in Congress. “I personally don’t think that there should be a doughnut hole,” he told me. But, he explained, “during the presidential campaign, Biden said that we should lift the cap over $400,000.” Larson came to understand why. “Every town hall I go to, I say, ‘Raise your hand if you’re making more than $400,000,’ and I’ve yet to have a hand go up.” As Democrats have become the party of the pretty-well-off, they have more and more voters who make more than $185,000 but less than $400,000. T he doughnut hole is one weakness, but every plan has its shortcomings. Last month, Senator Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat, and Senator Bernie Moreno, a Republican, announced a proposal to uncap the payroll tax entirely. Their idea gets points for bipartisanship, but it would close just two-thirds of the shortfall, at most, if implemented. Within three years, benefits would again exceed revenues. A few plans get the math to work out with sufficiently large tax hikes. Proposals by Larson and Senators Bernie Sanders and Sheldon Whitehouse would uncap the payroll tax (with a doughnut hole) and raise taxes on investment income. These are large tax increases: A wealthy self-employed couple making $800,000—imagine married doctors in private practice—would face a marginal federal tax rate of more than 50 percent. Perhaps the biggest problem with these plans is not the new taxes—it’s what the new taxes pay for. Conceived as an anti-poverty program, Social Security today pays out most of its benefits not to the poor but to the middle class and the rich. The bottom 20 percent of older Americans receive just 7 percent of the total benefits. The economics of old age have changed a lot since the 1930s. The prices of assets—homes and stocks—have soared. As a result of these trends, the elderly are now America’s wealthiest age group, living without children in homes that are on average roomier than everyone else’s. No longer are most of them financially dependent on their children; if anything, their descendants are more likely to ask them for help. [ Jessica Riedl: The elixir of the payroll-tax cap and other social security myths ] If the public craves a trillion-dollar tax increase on the well-off, there are better ways to spend the money. The federal government already spends about six times as much for each elderly adult as it does each child, even though children and their parents are more than twice as likely to be poor . Uncapping the payroll tax would not close the Social Security shortfall, but it would provide enough money to pay an allowance of $4,500 a child to every family in the country every year. Reforming Social Security by cutting benefits for rich people and raising them for poor people would make the most financial sense and return the program to its roots. President Franklin D. Roosevelt designed Social Security for the elderly not because they were uniquely deserving but because, as he put it in his signing address, they were so “poverty-ridden.” But that scenario—flattening Social Security and focusing future generosity on the young—doesn’t seem particularly likely. Social Security’s popularity comes from its universality, and if well-off people see their benefits reduced, the program could lose some of its widespread appeal. As I reported this story, many wonks recited to me the old political adage, “Programs for the poor make poor programs.” Indeed, most voters, when faced with the choice, prefer tax increases to benefit cuts. A poll last year by the National Academy of Social Insurance, a network of social-safety-net researchers, showed that the three most popular reform options were slightly different ways to uncap the payroll tax. The next most popular was an increase in benefits. Even raising the payroll-tax rate on everyone by one percentage point ranked above cutting benefits only for the rich. “Broad-based bipartisan majorities say that they’d even be willing to chip in a little bit more themselves,” Rebecca Vallas, the head of NASI, told me, “if that’s what it takes to prevent benefit cuts.” A social safety net that so heavily favors the old and rich over the needy and young is probably not what most Americans would design if they were starting from scratch, but it’s what we have. The likeliest outcome of whatever Congress comes up with in the next six years is that same dynamic, only more so.

items.title: Moyen-Orient : pourquoi les bases américaines sont vulnérables aux frappes iraniennes
items.description: Les sites militaires des Etats-Unis, installés dans la région à l’époque des guerres en Irak et en Afghanistan, sont particulièrement exposés aux attaques de drones et de missiles de Téhéran, qui arrivent désormais à en trouver les failles.

items.title: Houet : le CSPS de Bossora lance le deuxième passage de la CPS+
items.description: Houet : le CSPS de Bossora lance le deuxième passage de la CPS+ Bossora, 24 juil. 2026 (AIB) – Le Centre de santé et de promotion sociale (CSPS) de Bossora, dans la commune de Satiri a lancé, le jeudi 23 juillet 2026, le deuxième passage de la Chimio-prévention du paludisme saisonnier (CPS+). Cette campagne, qui se poursuivra jusqu'au 26 juillet, fait suite à une session de recyclage des distributeurs communautaires (DC) organisée le 22 juillet. Pendant ces quatre jours, les distributeurs communautaires effectueront des visites à domicile afin d'administrer les médicaments de la CPS+ aux enfants âgés de 3 à 59 mois, tranche d'âge ciblée par l'intervention. Ils procéderont également au dépistage de la malnutrition, à la recherche de cas d'œdèmes, à l'identification et à la destruction des gîtes larvaires, ainsi qu'au recensement des enfants sous-vaccinés et de ceux n'ayant reçu aucune dose de vaccin (« zéro dose »). Les équipes mèneront en outre des séances de sensibilisation sur les mesures de prévention du paludisme. Au total, douze équipes ont été déployées dans dix-huit hameaux de culture de l'aire sanitaire de Bossora. Elles ont pour objectif de protéger 1 927 enfants âgés de 3 à 59 mois contre le paludisme au cours de cette campagne. L'infirmier chef de poste du CSPS de Bossora, Ambroise Nabaloum, a exhorté les distributeurs communautaires à faire preuve d'engagement, de courage et de vigilance afin d'assurer le plein succès de l'opération. Il a souligné que la période des travaux champêtres constitue un défi majeur, de nombreuses familles se rendant quotidiennement aux champs avec leurs enfants. Le Camarade Nabaloum a également demandé l'accompagnement des parents ainsi que des autorités coutumières et religieuses, afin qu'ils réservent un bon accueil aux équipes de distribution et facilitent leur passage, pour permettre à tous les enfants éligibles de bénéficier de la Chimio-prévention du paludisme saisonnier, une stratégie essentielle pour réduire les cas de paludisme chez les jeunes enfants pendant la saison des pluies. Agence d’information du Burkina AAT/AB/wis
